topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to bleed across my vision. I stared at the 'D-'. My hand, gripping the edge of the desk, began to shake almost imperceptibly. It felt like a physical weight settling onto my chest, making every breath shallow. I reread the feedback, the professor’s critique sharp and unforgiving, each word a tiny shard of glass under my skin. I wanted to slam the paper down, to rip the evaluation in half, but my fingers remained frozen above the surface. A low, tight knot formed just beneath my sternum, radiating outwards. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafening in the sudden quiet of the study room.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the lecture in my head, searching for the moment I’d misread the assignment, the misplaced semicolon that derailed my entire argument. A cold sweat prickled along my hairline, dampening the collar of my shirt. My palms were slick; I consciously tried to press them flat against the wooden surface of the table, a futile attempt to anchor myself. When I finally looked up to read the score, the numbers swam slightly. A deep furrow appeared between my brows, pulling my features into a mask of strained concentration. "This isn't right," I whispered, the sound barely audible, thick with disbelief. I picked up my pen, but the tip hovered over the blank page, refusing to commit. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming inside my ears.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights above the library hummed with an irritating, relentless buzz, mirroring the agitation churning inside me. I traced the lines of the grade with my index finger, the sensation oddly abrasive. My jaw felt locked, muscles clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my neck. Every muscle in my body seemed coiled, ready to spring, yet I was paralyzed. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. When I finally managed to pull myself away from the desk, the movement was jerky. I walked toward the exit, my steps measured and unnaturally slow, acutely aware of every sound—the rustle of paper, the distant chatter—as if the world itself were holding its breath, waiting for the inevitable disappointment to fully materialize.
